message.content:
Adversaries may exploit the Microsoft Application Virtualization Injector (Mavinject) to proxy the execution of malicious code, leveraging its capability to inject code into external processes. This technique, known as System Binary Proxy Execution: Mavinject, involves injecting malicious DLLs into running processes, facilitating arbitrary code execution. Mavinject, being a legitimate and digitally signed Windows utility, can mask malicious activities under a trusted process, thereby evading detection by security products. Additionally, Mavinject can perform import descriptor injection using its /HMODULE command-line parameter, further enhancing its ability to manipulate process memory.

The potential impact includes unauthorized code execution, persistence, and evasion of security defenses. Common adversary tactics involve using Mavinject for Dynamic-link Library Injection and import descriptor injection to stealthily execute malicious payloads.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting the use of Mavinject, implementing strict application whitelisting policies, and employing behavioral analysis to detect unusual injection activities. Regularly updating and patching systems can also reduce the risk of exploitation.
